seoTitle: Sex Positions for Lower Back Pain: A Real Guide
seoDescription: The best sex positions for lower back pain depend on your spine, not one rule. Learn the flexion vs extension trick and find yours.
h1: Sex Positions for Lower Back Pain, Without the Useless Advice
dek: There are positions that work with your back instead of against it. The catch: the right one depends on your specific spine, and almost everyone gets handed the wrong answer first.

body.text: If you have searched this phrase, you have probably already gotten the standard reply, which is a vague, slightly embarrassed suggestion to spoon and maybe a pamphlet. The person who treats your back does not talk about sex, and the person who talks about sex does not know what an annulus is, so you end up in the gap between two professions that have apparently agreed never to be in the same room. We would like to close that gap. Carefully.

Here is the thing almost no one tells you: there is no universal best position for a bad back. There is a best position for your bad back, and figuring out which one means answering a question your doctor probably skipped. So let us actually answer it.
Why "just spoon" is bad advice for a lot of people

For years the blanket recommendation for back pain and sex was side-lying, the spoon, the safe choice. It sounds reasonable. It is gentle, it is low effort, it is the position your back has when you are asleep and not in trouble. The problem is that researchers eventually did the unglamorous thing and measured what actually happens to the lumbar spine during sex, and the tidy advice fell apart.

In 2014, Sidorkewicz and McGill documented lumbar spine motion across common positions and ranked them by how they load the spine. The takeaway was not a single winning position. It was that the same position can be a relief for one body and a slow-motion flare for another, depending on which direction your back hates. Spooning, for a meaningful share of people, is the slow-motion flare. That is a tough thing to learn after you bought it as the responsible option.

The best position for your back is not the one that is gentlest in theory. It is the one that keeps your spine out of the shape that hurts you. Those are not always the same thing, which is the entire problem.
The one idea that actually sorts this out: which direction does your back hate?

Almost every useful decision about your back, in bed or out of it, comes down to your directional preference. There are two big camps, and you very likely already know which one you are in even if no one ever gave it a name.

items: Flexion-intolerant: you feel worse sitting for a long time, slumping on the couch, bending forward to tie your shoes, or rolling out of a low car. Rounding the lower back is the enemy. This tracks with what we know about pressure inside the disc, which climbs when you sit and bend forward and eases when you stand or lie down.

Extension-intolerant: you feel worse standing for a long time, arching back, reaching overhead, or lying face-down. Compressing the back of the spine is the enemy, so anything that cranks you into a deep arch is trouble.

Not sure yet: spend a day noticing when it barks. Worse after a long sit means probably flexion-intolerant. Worse after a long stand or a big stretch backward means probably extension-intolerant. Your back has been telling you for months. We just gave it vocabulary.

body.text: Once you know your camp, the bedroom stops being a guessing game and starts being a logic problem with a pretty good payoff.
What this means once the lights are off

If you are flexion-intolerant, your spine wants to stay neutral or in a slight, comfortable extension. Positions that let you keep a small natural curve in the lower back tend to feel best, and a rolled towel under the right spot can keep you out of the slump your back resents. The instinct to curl in and protect yourself is usually the exact shape that bites you, which is a cruel little joke your nervous system likes to play.

If you are extension-intolerant, the priority is to not arch. Supported, side-lying, and slightly flexed shapes are your friends; deep arching positions are the ones to skip, even the famous ones, even the ones the internet swears by. Pillows are not a consolation prize here, they are equipment.

Same position, opposite verdict. The shape that rescues a flexion-intolerant back can be the one that wrecks an extension-intolerant one. This is why generic lists fail you and why one honest question beats a ranked listicle every time.
It also depends on your role, which is the part everyone skips

The research did not just rank positions, it ranked them by what each partner is actually doing. The person on top is not loading their spine the way the person underneath is, and the person moving is not in the same situation as the person being still. So a position can be a genuine gift for one of you and a quiet trap for the other, at the exact same moment, in the exact same bed.

This is why the conversation matters as much as the mechanics. You are not picking one winning position for the household. You are each finding the version that keeps your own spine happy, then meeting in the overlap. The overlap is real, and it is usually bigger than people fear it is. Most couples assume the choice is pain or intimacy. It is almost never actually that binary.
A few principles that travel with you

Stuart McGill's broader work is the quiet backbone of all of this, and it boils down to a handful of ideas worth keeping in your pocket. Endurance beats range of motion; a stable spine that holds its shape is safer than a bendy one that goes wherever it is pushed. Avoid repeated end-range loading, which is the technical way of saying do not keep slamming your spine into the position it hates. And the same daily habits that protect your back at a desk, on a walk, and getting out of bed are the ones that protect it here too.

items: Use props without shame. A pillow or rolled towel placed to hold your neutral shape is the cheapest, most effective thing in this whole article, and it costs roughly nothing.
Brace gently before you move, the way you would protect your back lifting a box. Bedrooms are not exempt from physics.
Stop treating the position as the only variable. Pace, support, and who is doing the work matter just as much.
Build a back that tolerates more, on purpose, with a short daily routine, so the menu of positions that feel fine gets longer over time instead of shorter.

body.text: None of this requires you to become a martyr or a monk. It requires one piece of information about your own spine and a willingness to ignore advice that was never built for your body in the first place. You are allowed to want both a back that works and a life that includes other people. Those are not competing requests.

faqs.q: What is the best sex position for lower back pain?
faqs.a: There is not one. The honest answer is that it depends on whether your back gets worse with bending and sitting (flexion-intolerant) or with arching and standing (extension-intolerant), and on which role you are in. A flexion-intolerant back wants neutral or slightly extended shapes; an extension-intolerant back wants to avoid arching and lean on supported, side-lying positions. Pick for your spine, not for a generic list.

faqs.q: Is spooning actually good for back pain?
faqs.a: Sometimes, and sometimes it is the thing quietly making you worse. When researchers measured spine motion across positions, side-lying was not a universal winner. For some people it keeps the back in a tolerable shape, for others it nudges it straight into the position that hurts. So spooning is worth trying, but it is not the safe default it gets sold as.

faqs.q: How do I know if my back is flexion-intolerant or extension-intolerant?
faqs.a: Notice when it flares. Worse after long sitting, slumping, or bending forward usually points to flexion-intolerant. Worse after long standing, arching back, or reaching overhead usually points to extension-intolerant. If you are torn, pay attention for a day or two; the pattern is usually clearer than you expect once you are actually looking for it.

faqs.q: Can sex make a herniated disc or back injury worse?
faqs.a: It can if you keep pushing your spine into the shape it does not tolerate, especially repeated end-range bending or arching. It does not have to. Staying near a neutral spine, using props for support, and choosing positions that match your directional preference all lower the load. If you have a known injury or new red-flag symptoms like leg weakness or numbness, check with a clinician first, ideally one who will actually answer the question.

faqs.q: What positions should I avoid with lower back pain?
faqs.a: Avoid whatever forces your back into the direction it hates and holds it there. If forward bending and sitting are your triggers, skip deeply rounded, slumped positions. If arching is your trigger, skip the deep-extension positions, even popular ones. The goal is not a long list of forbidden moves, it is staying out of one specific shape: yours.

faqs.q: Do pillows or props really help?
faqs.a: Yes, more than almost anything else here. A pillow or rolled towel placed to support your natural curve keeps your spine out of the position that triggers pain, with zero athleticism required. It is the least glamorous tip in this article and probably the most useful, which is true of a lot of good advice about backs.

sources: Sidorkewicz NM, McGill SM. Documenting the typical lumbar spine motion during sexual activity. Eur Spine J. 2014.
McGill SM. Low Back Disorders: Evidence-Based Prevention and Rehabilitation. (Endurance over range of motion; the Big 3; spine-sparing movement; avoiding repeated end-range loading.)
Nachemson A; later Wilke et al. In vivo measurements of intradiscal pressure showing posture-dependent disc loading (sitting and forward bending load the disc more than standing or lying).
rankerCta: Two questions, no pamphlet: take the free ranker and it will score the positions against your actual spine instead of someone else's.
